# **A Three-Phase Deep Reinforcement Learning Architecture for the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits**

## **1\. Introduction and Theoretical Foundations**

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a cornerstone of combinatorial optimization, operations research, and applied mathematics. Since its initial formulation by Dantzig and Ramser, the VRP has evolved into a vast taxonomy of highly constrained sub-problems designed to model the complexities of modern supply chain logistics, telecommunications routing, and autonomous agent navigation.1 Among the most computationally demanding variants is the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP), which frequently intersects with formulations known as the Orienteering Problem (OP), the Team Orienteering Problem (TOP), and the Capacitated Profitable Tour Problem (CPTP).3

In standard VRP formulations, the objective is strictly to minimize the total routing cost (usually distance or travel time) required to service a fixed, immutable set of customer nodes.6 The VRPP introduces a profound structural complication: the solver is no longer required to visit every node. Instead, the graph is partitioned into a set of mandatory nodes (which strictly demand service) and a set of optional nodes (which offer a discrete profit if visited).5 Consequently, the fundamental objective function undergoes a paradigm shift from pure cost minimization to the maximization of a scalar metric representing the collected profits minus the routing expenditures.3

This dual-objective nature transforms the VRPP into a simultaneous synthesis of the Knapsack Problem (a subset selection task) and the Traveling Salesperson Problem (a sequence optimization task). Because both fundamental problems are strongly NP-hard, their intersection in the VRPP generates a combinatorial explosion that rapidly overwhelms exact operations research methodologies.12 Exact solvers utilizing Branch-and-Cut, Branch-and-Price, or rigorous Column Generation procedures suffer from exponential time degradation and frequently fail to converge on instances exceeding 100 nodes within operational time horizons.12 To circumvent these computational bottlenecks, the logistics industry and academic community have historically relied upon metaheuristics such as Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Genetic Algorithms (GA).6 While these algorithms provide robust approximate solutions, they are entirely reliant on handcrafted, problem-specific local search operators that struggle to adapt to shifting node distributions, real-time graph dynamism, or non-Euclidean cost matrices.16

The advent of Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) has fundamentally disrupted this landscape. By framing routing tasks as sequential Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), researchers have successfully trained Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agents to generate high-quality solutions in a fraction of the inference time required by classical heuristics.12 However, the deployment of end-to-end, single-phase DRL models for the VRPP has revealed a critical algorithmic vulnerability: catastrophic interference. When a single neural network is forced to simultaneously learn a knapsack-like subset selection policy (maximizing profit) and a TSP-like routing policy (minimizing distance), the reward gradients frequently oppose one another. This opposition leads to suboptimal weight updates, highly variant training curves, and the ultimate failure of the policy network to respect rigid mathematical constraints.20

To resolve this algorithmic bottleneck, current research advocates for the structural decomposition of the problem. Drawing inspiration from Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) and discrete divide-and-conquer methodologies 20, this report defines a rigorous, exhaustively detailed Three-Phase Deep Reinforcement Learning Architecture designed explicitly to solve the VRPP.

## **2\. Mathematical Formulation of the VRPP**

To establish the necessary academic rigor for the subsequent neural network designs, the VRPP must first be formalized as an Integer Linear Program (ILP). The accuracy of the DRL reward functions relies entirely on an exact mathematical representation of the problem space and its constraints.

### **2.1 Graph Theory and Parameter Definitions**

The VRPP is defined over a complete directed graph ![][image1]. Let ![][image2] represent the set of vertices, where ![][image3] denotes the central origin and destination depot, ![][image4] denotes the set of mandatory customer nodes, and ![][image5] denotes the set of optional customer nodes.5

Each node ![][image6] is characterized by a tuple of deterministic parameters:

* ![][image7]: The profit collected upon visiting node ![][image8] (where ![][image9] for ![][image10] if the objective only scales optional profit, or ![][image11] to heavily weight mandatory inclusion).
* ![][image12]: The required service time at node ![][image8].
* ![][image13]: The physical load demand required at node ![][image8].

Let ![][image14] represent the edge set. Each directed edge ![][image15] incurs a travel cost ![][image16] (often distance or fuel expenditure) and a travel time ![][image17]. It is critical to avoid the Euclidean assumption; in real-world road networks, ![][image18] due to asymmetric traffic patterns and one-way thoroughfares.16

A fleet of ![][image19] homogeneous vehicles is stationed at the depot. Each vehicle is constrained by a maximum physical payload capacity ![][image20] and a maximum operational route duration ![][image21].5

### **2.2 Decision Variables**

The optimization model requires three sets of decision variables to map the routing sequences and the subset selections:

1. **Routing Variables:** ![][image22], where ![][image23] if vehicle ![][image24] traverses edge ![][image25], and 0 otherwise.
2. **Selection Variables:** ![][image26], where ![][image27] if node ![][image28] is visited by vehicle ![][image24], and 0 otherwise.
3. **State Variables:** ![][image29], a continuous variable representing the accumulated load of vehicle ![][image30] immediately upon departing node ![][image8]. This variable is essential for subtour elimination.

### **2.3 The Integer Linear Programming (ILP) Objective and Constraints**

The objective function of the VRPP seeks to maximize the net systemic profit, formulated as the total collected optional profits minus the total routing expenditures, scaled by a balancing parameter ![][image31]:

![][image32]
This objective function is subject to a rigid framework of mathematical constraints that dictate topological and physical feasibility:

**1\. Mandatory Node Fulfillment:** Every mandatory node must be serviced exactly once by exactly one vehicle in the fleet. This is an absolute equality constraint.5

![][image33]
**2\. Optional Node Restriction:** Every optional node may be visited at most once. The solver may choose to leave optional nodes unvisited if the routing cost outweighs the profit.3

![][image34]
**3\. Flow Conservation and Degree Constraints:** If a vehicle arrives at a node, it must subsequently depart from that exact node. This guarantees continuous, unbroken trajectories.9

![][image35]
![][image36]
**4\. Depot Origination and Termination:** Each active vehicle must initiate its route at the depot and terminate its route at the depot.5

![][image37]
![][image38]
**5\. Payload Capacity and Subtour Elimination:** Adapted from the foundational Miller-Tucker-Zemlin (MTZ) formulation, these constraints strictly enforce the maximum payload ![][image20] and simultaneously prevent the formation of disconnected, floating subtours in the graph space. ![][image39] represents an arbitrarily large constant (Big-M method).9

![][image40]
![][image41]
**6\. Maximum Route Duration Horizon:** The cumulative sum of travel times and service times for any single vehicle must not exceed the operational horizon limits ![][image21].5

![][image42]

### **2.4 The Failure of Exact Linear Relaxations**

Attempting to solve the aforementioned ILP using classical LP-relaxations (where $x\_{ijk} \\in $) yields highly fragmented fractional solutions. The bound quality produced by Dantzig-Wolfe decomposition or Lagrangian relaxation degrades sharply as the ratio of ![][image43] to ![][image44] increases.12 The inherent nonlinearity of the capacity constraint further complicates the geometry of the feasible polyhedra, rendering traditional branch-and-cut algorithms entirely impractical for real-time or near-real-time deployment in VRPP contexts.12 This intractable complexity explicitly justifies the necessity of neural approximations.

## **3\. The Paradigm Shift: DRL in Combinatorial Optimization**

The integration of Deep Reinforcement Learning into combinatorial optimization has polarized the methodologies into two distinct architectural schools: Construction-Type Models and Improvement-Type Models. Understanding the mathematical and structural differences between these two paradigms is vital, as the proposed 3-phase architecture synergistically combines both to exploit their respective strengths.20

### **3.1 Construction-Type Models**

Construction-type DRL models treat the routing task as a strictly sequential autoregressive generation process. Operating from an empty state ![][image45], the policy network sequentially selects the next unvisited node to append to the tour until all constraints are met or all nodes are visited.

These models heavily utilize encoder-decoder architectures relying on Attention Mechanisms. The seminal Attention Model (AM) and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) paved the way for more sophisticated topologies, such as the Local-Global Feature Fusion Transformer (FusionFormer) and Pointer Networks.26 The FusionFormer, for instance, integrates a Distance-Assisted Multi-Head Attention (DA-MHA) mechanism in the encoder to process the spatial geometry, combined with a Proximity-Guided Attention (PGA) decoder to ensure that sequentially selected nodes belong to logical spatial clusters.

The MDP formulation for construction models dictates that the policy network ![][image46] learns to factorize the joint probability of constructing a complete route ![][image47] given an input graph ![][image48] via the mathematical chain rule:

![][image49]
The primary advantage of construction models is their inference velocity; once the network weights ![][image50] are trained via policy gradients (e.g., REINFORCE), evaluating a new graph requires only a single forward pass, making it highly suitable for real-time deployment.20 However, their greedy, autoregressive nature restricts their look-ahead capabilities, meaning they frequently become trapped in local optima or are forced to execute massive detours at the end of a sequence to satisfy capacity constraints.26

### **3.2 Improvement-Type Models**

In contrast, improvement-type models abandon the concept of sequential generation. Instead, they require an initial, fully complete (and mathematically feasible) solution. The DRL agent then explores the neighborhood of this solution by learning to apply local search operators—such as 2-opt, 3-opt, node-swap, or sequence relocation—iteratively.28

A premier example in recent literature is the Synergetic Attention-driven Transformer (SAT), which leverages a cyclic mapping strategy. Because vehicle routes begin and end at the depot, they are inherently circular permutations. SAT utilizes Cyclic Positional Encoding to capture this circularity, allowing the Transformer to maintain permutation equivariance across the cyclic state space.20

These improvement models are predominantly trained using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), which guarantees monotonic policy improvement by bounding the gradient updates.7 While improvement models consistently outperform construction models and classical heuristics in solution precision (often by margins exceeding 8.56%) and exhibit superior generalization to out-of-distribution graph sizes, they exact a heavy computational toll. Executing thousands of iterative rollouts through a massive Transformer network drastically increases the inference latency, precluding their use in ultra-low-latency environments without extensive hardware parallelization.19

### **3.3 The Comparative Topography**

To contextualize the architectural decisions made in the subsequent sections, the performance characteristics of these two paradigms are strictly compared in the table below.

| Architectural Feature | Construction-Type Models | Improvement-Type Models |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Primary Mechanism** | Sequentially appends unvisited nodes to build a route from an empty set. | Iteratively refines a complete initial feasible solution using discrete operators. |
| **Key Frameworks** | Attention Model (AM), FusionFormer, Pointer Networks, DACT. | Synergetic Attention-driven Transformer (SAT), DeepLS, WADRL. |
| **Optimization Algorithm** | Autoregressive Policy Gradient (e.g., REINFORCE with baseline). | Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C). |
| **Primary Strengths** | Ultra-fast inference latency; suitable for instantaneous real-time deployment. | Vastly superior solution quality; robust generalization to unseen graph scales. |
| **Primary Limitations** | Highly susceptible to local optima; struggles with strict capacity bounding late in the sequence. | High computational overhead; slow convergence; requires a valid initial state. |

For the highly complex VRPP, utilizing a single paradigm is insufficient. Construction models fail to select the mathematically optimal knapsack subset of profitable nodes, while improvement models cannot function without a high-quality initial routing state. Therefore, a hybrid, multi-stage decoupling is strictly required.20

## **4\. Architecture Overview: The 3-Phase DRL Framework**

To eliminate the catastrophic interference generated by simultaneous profit maximization and distance minimization, the proposed architecture dismantles the VRPP into three sequentially executed, mathematically isolated domains. This decoupling ensures that the reward landscapes of each neural agent are aligned with a single, stationary objective, dramatically reducing the variance in the policy gradient estimation.

This tripartite structure mirrors the advanced methodologies observed in state-of-the-art literature, such as the Double-Level DRL (DL-DRL) proposed for multi-UAV task scheduling and the Threshold Acceptance-Based Multi-Layer Search (TAMLS) framework for capacitated routing.20 By separating the decision boundaries, the architecture ensures that topological constraints are strictly maintained throughout the pipeline.

### **4.1 System Topology**

| Phase Hierarchy | Core Optimization Objective | Deployed DRL Paradigm | Algorithmic Mechanism & Architecture | Output |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Phase 1: Node Selection** | Identify the mathematically optimal subset of profitable nodes. | Upper-Level HRL / Token-Specific DRL | Graph Attention Networks (GAT), Binary Node Classification | Active Graph ![][image51] |
| **Phase 2: Route Construction** | Generate a high-quality, constraint-compliant initial feasible tour. | Autoregressive Policy Gradient (REINFORCE) | Encoder-Decoder Transformer, Pointer Network, Dynamic Masking | Initial Route Matrix ![][image52] |
| **Phase 3: Route Improvement** | Escape local optima via continuous neighborhood exploration. | DRL-Augmented Local Search (DeepLS) | Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Cyclic Positional Encoding | Optimized Route Matrix ![][image53] |

This hierarchical decomposition allows each phase to act as a rigorous filter for the subsequent phase. Phase 1 strips away topological noise by rejecting unprofitable optional nodes. Phase 2 leverages this purified graph to build a mathematically valid trajectory without the burden of evaluating profit trade-offs. Phase 3 injects non-stationary refinement, allowing the system to continuously audit and improve the sequence until computational time expires.20

## **5\. Phase 1: Token-Specific DRL for Node Selection**

The fundamental objective of Phase 1 is to evaluate the global graph ![][image54] and deterministically select a subset of optional nodes, ![][image55], that maximizes the expected systemic profit without violating the theoretical payload capacity ![][image20] and time horizon ![][image21] of the entire vehicle fleet.

### **5.1 Token-Specific Graph Representation**

Standard node embedding techniques treat all vertices homogeneously, creating latent vectors that fail to distinguish between the critical roles of a depot, a mandatory customer, or an optional profit center. To resolve this, Phase 1 operates via a Token-Specific Deep Reinforcement Learning (TS-DRL) framework.20 TS-DRL injects highly specific token characteristics directly into the continuous latent representation of each node, dynamically adapting the evaluation policy based on node-specific metadata.

Let the initial, raw feature vector for node ![][image8] be defined as ![][image56]. The categorical parameter ![][image57] acts as the token identifier, explicitly denoting whether the node is the depot, mandatory, or optional. These raw features are linearly projected into a high-dimensional continuous latent space (![][image58]):

![][image59]
To capture the complex spatial, temporal, and economic relationships across the graph—for instance, recognizing that a tight cluster of low-profit optional nodes might be collectively more efficient to service than a single, distant high-profit node—the architecture utilizes a Multi-Head Attention (MHA) Graph Neural Network (GNN) encoder.20

For each attention head ![][image60], the query, key, and value tensors are computed via learned weight matrices:

![][image61]
Crucially, the standard dot-product attention must be augmented to process the edge costs ![][image62] (distance or time) directly. Failure to inject edge features results in a severe loss of topological awareness, particularly in non-Euclidean environments.7 The token-specific attention weights are therefore calculated as:

![][image63]
The output embeddings ![][image64] produced after ![][image65] attention layers contain a rich, deeply contextualized representation of the node's individual profit value weighed against its geographic isolation and the surrounding topological constraints.

### **5.2 The Selection Policy MDP**

The node selection process is formulated mathematically as an episodic Markov Decision Process (MDP) operating specifically over the set of optional nodes ![][image5].

* **State Space ![][image66]:** The state ![][image67] at evaluation step ![][image68] consists of the global graph embeddings ![][image69], an aggregate representation of the current remaining fleet capacity ![][image70], the remaining global time budget ![][image71], and a binary mask vector ![][image72] indicating which optional nodes have already been evaluated and processed.
* **Action Space ![][image73]:** The action space is strictly binary. The action ![][image74] dictates whether the currently attended optional node ![][image8] is added to ![][image75].
* **Transition Dynamics ![][image76]:** Upon taking an action, the state transitions deterministically. If ![][image77] (node selected), the expected systemic capacity and time budgets are heuristically decremented based on the node's demand ![][image78] and the minimum spanning tree approximation of travel time. If ![][image79] (node rejected), the budgets remain unchanged. In both cases, the mask vector ![][image80] is updated to mark node ![][image8] as evaluated.
* **Reward Function ![][image81]:** The step reward directly reflects the objective function. ![][image82] if ![][image77]. However, if the total cumulative demand of the selected nodes exceeds the absolute fleet bounds (![][image83]), a massive terminal penalty ![][image84] is applied, completely destroying the episode's reward and forcing the policy network to learn conservative, mathematically safe selection thresholds.

The policy network ![][image85] is trained utilizing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), ensuring that the updates to the selection thresholds remain stable.7

**Handling Mandatory Nodes:** A defining feature of the Phase 1 architecture is its handling of mandatory nodes ![][image4]. Rather than relying on the stochastic policy network to learn to include them (which risks constraint violations if the network outputs a low probability), the inclusion of ![][image4] is hardcoded as an absolute mathematical imperative.5 Their inclusion probability is deterministically set to ![][image86]. The MDP merely updates the initial state budgets ![][image70] and ![][image71] by subtracting the demands of ![][image4] before evaluating any node in ![][image5].

The final output of Phase 1 is the construction of an active, highly profitable routing graph ![][image87], where the active vertices are defined as ![][image88].

## **6\. Phase 2: Route Construction**

With the active graph ![][image89] firmly defined and stripped of unprofitable topological noise, Phase 2 is tasked with generating an initial, feasible route matrix ![][image52]. Because Phase 1 has already solved the subset selection sub-problem, the Phase 2 agent is faced with a much smoother, pure routing optimization landscape, allowing it to focus entirely on distance minimization and capacity sequencing.

### **6.1 Autoregressive Encoder-Decoder Architecture**

Phase 2 treats route construction as an autoregressive sequence generation task. Operating identically to a natural language processing model predicting the next token in a sentence, the DRL agent must factor the joint probability of a complete route ![][image90] via the probabilistic chain rule 19:

![][image91]
The architecture utilizes a Dual-Aspect Collaborative Transformer (DACT).20 DACT architectures are highly preferred for routing tasks because they compute embeddings for static node features (coordinates, total demand) separately from dynamic positional features (the current load of the vehicle, the remaining time). Fusing these inputs prematurely creates incompatible correlations and topological noise. By keeping them separated in a dual-stream encoder until the final decoding step, DACT prevents the corruption of the spatial geometry during the sequential generation process.20

### **6.2 The Decoding Mechanism and Pointer Network**

At decoding step ![][image68], the network must decide which node in ![][image92] to visit next. To accomplish this, the decoder constructs a dynamic context vector ![][image93]. This vector is a concatenation of the global graph embedding ![][image94] (the mean pooling of all node embeddings), the embedding of the depot ![][image95], and the embedding of the currently occupied node ![][image96].

A context query vector ![][image97] is generated via a linear projection of ![][image93]. This query is then projected against the key matrices ![][image98] of all nodes in the graph to calculate a raw compatibility score (logit) using a Pointer Network mechanism:

![][image99]
Here, ![][image100] is a scaling hyperparameter (typically 10\) that prevents the logits from exploding and saturating the gradients. The raw scores are passed through a Softmax activation function to generate a valid probability distribution over the available nodes:

![][image101]

### **6.3 Dynamic Masking and Feasibility Auditing**

A systemic flaw in early end-to-end DRL routing algorithms is the reliance on "soft" penalty enforcement. In these flawed systems, the neural network is permitted to select an invalid node (e.g., a node whose demand exceeds the vehicle's remaining capacity), and the environment subsequently punishes the network with a large negative reward. This approach is computationally destructive; it forces the DRL agent to memorize mathematical tautologies rather than learning structural optimization strategies, often resulting in models that still output invalid routes in 5-10% of their inferences.12

To ensure absolute adherence to the VRPP ILP constraints defined in Section 2, Phase 2 implements a rigorous, deterministic **Dynamic Masking Mechanism**. Let ![][image102] be the current accumulated payload of the vehicle, and ![][image103] be the current elapsed time at step ![][image68]. An unvisited node ![][image104] is mathematically masked and forced to a probability of zero (![][image105]) if taking that action would violate a hard constraint:

1. **Capacity Violation:** ![][image106]. The node's demand exceeds the available space.
2. **Time Horizon Violation:** ![][image107]. The travel time to the node, plus the required service time, plus the mandatory travel time to return to the depot exceeds the maximum horizon. The required return leg (![][image108]) must be calculated continuously to prevent vehicles from being stranded in the graph.

If all remaining customer nodes in ![][image92] fail the feasibility audit at step ![][image68], the only valid, unmasked action is to return to the depot (![][image109]). Upon selecting the depot, the vehicle state is reset (![][image110], ![][image111]), a new vehicle from the fleet ![][image19] is deployed, and the decoding process resumes.

### **6.4 Policy Gradient Training (REINFORCE)**

The Phase 2 network parameters ![][image112] are updated using the REINFORCE algorithm augmented with a deterministic rollout baseline to minimize the variance of the gradient estimators.20

Let ![][image113] be the objective cost of the generated route. Because Phase 1 already optimized the collected profit, Phase 2 defines ![][image113] strictly as the total routing distance or travel time. The loss gradient is mathematically formulated as:

![][image114]
The baseline scalar ![][image115] represents the cost of a route generated by a deterministic, greedy execution of the current best-performing historical policy. If the actively training stochastic policy consistently outperforms the deterministic baseline over a validation epoch, the baseline network weights are updated via a paired, one-sided T-test. This mechanism ensures monotonic improvement of the construction algorithm.

## **7\. Phase 3: Route Improvement**

While Phase 2 rapidly constructs a mathematically valid, constraint-compliant, and highly competent initial tour (![][image52]), construction heuristics inherently suffer from myopia. The autoregressive chain process lacks global look-ahead capabilities, frequently resulting in crossed edges, suboptimal terminal insertions, or inefficient vehicle utilization at the end of the decoding sequence.12

Phase 3 serves as the crucial refinement layer, executing DRL-augmented Local Search (DeepLS).29 In this phase, the DRL agent abandons sequential generation; instead, it acts as a highly intelligent heuristic selector, navigating the complex neighborhood of the solution space to continuously refine the sequence.

### **7.1 DRL-Augmented Local Search (DeepLS)**

Improvement models iteratively refine a complete topological sequence using established operations research operators. The Phase 3 architecture is fundamentally structured around learning *which* operator to apply, and *where* to apply it, based on the current state of the route.20

* **State Space ![][image116]:** The state ![][image117] at iteration ![][image30] is the complete, multi-vehicle sequence of the current route ![][image118]. Because vehicle routes necessarily begin and end at the depot, the mathematical structure of the solution is a cyclic sequence. Standard Transformers degrade when processing cyclic structures because positional encodings (sine/cosine) assume linear beginnings and ends. To resolve this, Phase 3 utilizes Cyclic Positional Encoding (CPE).20 CPE injects circular continuous geometry into the embeddings, ensuring the network recognizes that the first node visited after leaving the depot is adjacent to the depot, just as the final node is.
* **Action Space ![][image119]:** The action space is discrete and multidimensional. An action consists of selecting an operator ![][image120] and selecting the precise node indices upon which the operator will act. The operator pool ![][image121] contains intra-route heuristics (e.g., 2-opt, 3-opt, node relocation) and inter-route heuristics (e.g., swapping a node between Vehicle A and Vehicle B to rebalance capacities).8
* **Transition Dynamics ![][image122]:** The transition is purely deterministic. The selected operator modifies the route, generating ![][image123].
* **Reward Function ![][image124]:** The step reward is the exact marginal improvement in the objective function: ![][image125]. If an operator application degrades the solution quality, the reward is strictly negative.

### **7.2 Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in Discrete Spaces**

Training the Phase 3 improvement policy requires exceptional sample efficiency and mathematical stability, making Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) the algorithm of choice.7 Standard policy gradients (like REINFORCE) are highly unstable in iterative improvement spaces; a single oversized weight update can collapse the policy into repeatedly selecting identical, useless operators.

PPO prevents these destructive policy collapses by bounding the probability ratio of the new policy to the old policy. Let ![][image126] denote this ratio:

![][image127]
The clipped surrogate objective function for Phase 3 is mathematically defined as:

![][image128]
Here, ![][image129] is the Generalized Advantage Estimator (GAE), representing how much better the chosen action was compared to the expected baseline value of that state. The hyperparameter ![][image130] (typically set to 0.2) strictly limits the step size of the stochastic gradient ascent. If the probability ratio exceeds ![][image131] or falls below ![][image132], the gradient is clipped, neutralizing the update. By combining this tightly bounded gradient ascent with the inherent capability of local search operators to escape local optima, Phase 3 relentlessly forces the routing solution toward the global optimum.20

### **7.3 Synergetic Node Injection and Ejection**

A profoundly unique element of Phase 3, explicitly tailored for the VRPP, is the dynamic re-evaluation of Phase 1 decisions. As Phase 3 utilizes 2-opt and swap operators to decrease the total routing distance, it inevitably creates "slack" in the capacity and time budgets of the vehicle fleet.

To exploit this newly recovered budget, Phase 3 incorporates a **Synergetic Injection/Ejection Operator**.8 The Phase 3 agent may choose an action that scans the pool of optional nodes that were previously rejected by Phase 1 (![][image133]). It evaluates the marginal insertion cost of these unvisited nodes against their potential profit ![][image134]. If a highly profitable node can now be inserted into the optimized tour without violating the MTZ or route duration constraints, the agent executes the insertion, simultaneously increasing the objective profit and updating the graph geometry.

Conversely, if a previously selected optional node is found to require a massive distance detour that negates its profit margin (an error in the Phase 1 heuristic projection), the agent can eject the node from the active graph. This bidirectional operator creates a powerful, non-stationary feedback loop that rectifies any heuristic myopia generated during the earlier phases, seamlessly blending the subset selection and routing tasks at the extreme limits of optimization.

## **8\. Implementation Strategies and Advanced Training Paradigms**

Training a three-phase DRL architecture consisting of millions of parameters is an exercise in extreme algorithmic orchestration. If Phase 1 provides highly erratic, non-sensical node subsets early in the training process, Phase 2 will be forced to train on garbage topological data, and the entire pipeline will fail to converge.

### **8.1 Curriculum Learning (CL) Framework**

To enforce stable, synergistic training across the three interconnected neural networks, the architecture must utilize a structured Curriculum Learning (CL) framework. CL scales the combinatorial complexity of the training instances progressively, allowing the networks to master foundational concepts before encountering dense constraints.7

The curriculum is divided into three distinct evolutionary stages:

* **Curriculum Phase A (Topological Simplicity):** The networks are initially trained on extremely small, dense graphs (![][image135]) where all nodes are designated as mandatory (![][image136]) and capacity constraints are set to infinity. In this environment, Phase 1 is functionally bypassed. Phase 2 and Phase 3 focus exclusively on learning standard TSP-level Euclidean routing representations, mastering the basics of distance minimization and sequence construction without the overwhelming noise of knapsack subset selection.7
* **Curriculum Phase B (Constraint Introduction):** Fleet capacity ![][image20] and maximum time limit constraints ![][image21] are aggressively introduced. The deterministic dynamic masking protocols in Phase 2 (Section 6.3) are activated. Phase 3 is forced to train on capacity-aware inter-route swaps, learning to balance loads between multiple vehicles.
* **Curriculum Phase C (The Complete VRPP Environment):** Optional nodes ![][image5] and highly heterogeneous profit distributions ![][image134] are introduced. Phase 1 is fully activated to learn the TS-DRL selection policy. The full tri-phase pipeline begins end-to-end execution, optimizing the dual-objective profit/distance function.7

The transition between these curricula must be governed by strict mathematical thresholds rather than arbitrary epoch counts. Curriculum advancement occurs exclusively when the rolling average of the validation optimality gap (compared against a highly optimized heuristic baseline like ALNS) falls below an acceptable epsilon boundary (e.g., ![][image137]).

## **9\. Critique and Advanced Theoretical Considerations**

While the proposed three-phase architecture resolves the most pressing limitations of end-to-end DRL routing models, an uncompromising academic audit reveals several structural vulnerabilities inherent to the broader field of Neural Combinatorial Optimization that must be addressed in future derivations.

### **9.1 The Fallacy of the Euclidean Assumption**

The vast majority of DRL routing models published in premier venues operate under the implicit assumption of symmetric, Euclidean travel costs, where the triangle inequality (![][image138]) universally holds true. This is a severe failure of academic grounding. Real-world physical networks are heavily asymmetric, characterized by one-way streets, dynamic traffic congestion, topology barriers, and speed variations.16

If the Graph Neural Network encoders in Phase 1 and Phase 2 rely solely on spatial coordinates ![][image139] rather than explicitly mapping a directed, asymmetric cost matrix, the latent embeddings will collapse upon physical deployment, resulting in wildly infeasible routes. The proposed architecture attempts to mitigate this by directly embedding the raw edge cost ![][image62] into the Multi-Head Attention mechanism (Section 5.1, Equation 3). However, this increases the computational complexity of the attention mechanism from a highly manageable ![][image140] to an intensive ![][image141]. While mathematically necessary, this complexity explosion presents severe scalability issues for massive graphs (![][image142]).

### **9.2 Inference Latency vs. Graph Dynamism**

Improvement-type models (Phase 3\) operate as iterative Markov chains.29 While they produce solutions of unparalleled quality, the requirement to execute thousands of local search rollouts through a massive DACT Transformer network creates immense inference latency. In highly dynamic urban logistics scenarios—such as the Dynamic VRPP (DVRPP)—where customer nodes appear or cancel orders stochastically, the network must be capable of instantaneous re-routing (latency ![][image143] seconds).35

Phase 3 routinely violates these strict computational time budgets. Deployment on highly parallelized hardware (GPUs/TPUs) provides a partial solution, but the SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) architecture of modern accelerators struggles with the heavy conditional branching required by local search feasibility checks.19

To resolve this in future work, Phase 1 must be upgraded to utilize Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Networks (STGCNs) or Recurrent Graph Neural Networks (RGNNs). These networks can maintain a temporal state memory, allowing them to dynamically re-score the profit-to-distance ratios of incoming stochastic nodes on the fly without triggering a complete recalculation of the entire Phase 2 and Phase 3 pipelines. Furthermore, integrating Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) or Value Function Approximation with Consensus Functions (VFA-CF) can augment the Phase 1 agent's ability to anticipate stochastic node arrivals based on historical probability distributions.38

### **9.3 Decentralization via Multi-Agent Swarm Logic**

As logistics networks scale toward drone swarms and autonomous multi-UAV environments, centralized dispatchers (like the Phase 2 sequence generator) create single points of failure and massive communication overheads.39 Future extensions of this architecture should pivot toward decentralized, Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) paradigms.

Specifically, Hierarchical Role-Based Multi-Agent DRL (HMUDRL) can be integrated into the VRPP.20 In an HMUDRL framework, the global fleet utilizes centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE). The agents share a centralized critic during training to learn cooperative load balancing and collision avoidance, but they execute completely decentralized actor policies during inference, drastically reducing the inference latency and improving network resilience.20

## **10\. Conclusions**

The Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits represents a mathematically unforgiving synthesis of the combinatorial knapsack problem and sequence optimization. Traditional single-phase Deep Reinforcement Learning models consistently fail to generalize over this domain due to the catastrophic interference generated by the fundamentally opposing reward gradients of profit maximization and distance minimization.

The proposed Three-Phase Deep Reinforcement Learning Architecture resolves this fundamental computational bottleneck by systematically and strictly decoupling the mathematical objectives. Phase 1 leverages Token-Specific DRL (TS-DRL) and Multi-Head Graph Attention to evaluate the economic and topological validity of individual nodes, establishing a high-value active graph while guaranteeing mandatory node fulfillment. Phase 2 isolates the routing objective entirely, utilizing an autoregressive Dual-Aspect Collaborative Transformer guided by rigorous, deterministic feasibility masking to synthesize a mathematically compliant initial tour. Finally, Phase 3 functions as a highly intelligent, non-stationary refinement engine, utilizing PPO-driven local search operators and a synergetic node injection mechanism to escape local optima and push the objective function toward the absolute boundaries of Pareto optimality.

By anchoring the neural learning mechanisms firmly within the rigid, uncompromising constraints of Operations Research, and completely replacing soft penalty heuristics with exact mathematical auditing, this three-phase architecture establishes a highly scalable, structurally profound, and theoretically sound paradigm for solving next-generation combinatorial optimization problems.

1. Deep Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Truck Vehicle Routing Problems with Multi-Leg Demand Routes \- arXiv, accessed April 16, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2401.08669v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2401.08669v1)
